title: Installing Packages
subtitle: Add other people's code to your project

sections.content:
You're about to install your first npm package: **Express**. Express is the most popular framework for building web servers in JavaScript. Over 30 million developers download it every week.

When you run `npm install`, three things happen:

1. npm downloads the package from the registry
2. It saves the code in a folder called `node_modules`
3. It records the package name in your `package.json` under dependencies

Make sure you're still inside your `my-server` folder before continuing.
sections.tip: If you're not sure which folder you're in, run pwd to check. You should see something ending in /my-server.

sections.instruction: Install Express. This downloads Express and all the packages it depends on into your project.

troubleshooting.problem: ENOENT no such file or directory, open package.json
troubleshooting.solution: You're not in the right folder. Run: cd my-server (or wherever you created the project), then try again.

sections.instruction: Now look at your `package.json` again. You should see a new `dependencies` section listing Express.
Let's peek inside `node_modules` — the folder where npm stores all downloaded packages. Express needs many other packages to work, so you'll see way more than just 'express' in there.

sections.content:
That's a lot of folders! Express depends on other packages, which depend on *other* packages. This is called the **dependency tree**.

Here's the important thing: you never commit `node_modules` to git. It can contain thousands of files and hundreds of megabytes. Instead, your `package.json` records what you need, and anyone who clones your project can run `npm install` to download everything fresh.

This is why `package.json` matters so much — it's the recipe. `node_modules` is the groceries.
sections.analogy: package.json is a shopping list. node_modules is the actual groceries. You share the list, not a truckload of food.
keyPoints: npm install downloads packages into node_modules/
package.json records which packages your project depends on
Never commit node_modules to git — it's too large
Anyone can recreate node_modules by running npm install

completionMessage: You installed your first package! Express is ready to use. But first, there's one important housekeeping step — making sure git ignores the files it shouldn't track.
